pillar.h1: Does Medicare Cover Zepbound? GLP-1 & Drug Coverage in 2026
pillar.quickAnswer: Medicare Part D does not cover Zepbound for weight loss alone, but may cover it for an approved medical use like obstructive sleep apnea. Starting July 1, 2026, a temporary CMS GLP-1 Bridge offers eligible enrollees Zepbound (KwikPen) for a $50 copay per 30-day supply.
pillar.intro: GLP-1 drugs like Zepbound, Ozempic, and Wegovy dominate headlines, but Medicare's rules are nuanced. Here is what is actually covered in 2026.
pillar.heroSubhead: Straight answers on Zepbound, Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro, insulin, and the 2026 weight-loss drug rules from licensed Medicare specialists at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: Why doesn't Medicare cover Zepbound for weight loss?
sections.body: By law, Medicare Part D is prohibited from covering any drug used only for weight loss. Zepbound (tirzepatide) is FDA-approved for chronic weight management, so a plan cannot pay for it when that is the sole reason. This restriction dates back to the 2003 Medicare Modernization Act and still applies in 2026. The workaround is medical indication: if Zepbound is prescribed for an approved condition Medicare does cover, such as moderate-to-severe obstructive sleep apnea in adults with obesity, a Part D plan that lists it on its formulary may pay. Coverage is always indication-specific, so the exact diagnosis on your prescription matters. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to confirm your plan's rules.

sections.h2: What is the 2026 Medicare GLP-1 Bridge program?
sections.body: Beginning July 1, 2026, CMS launched a temporary demonstration nicknamed the GLP-1 Bridge that, for the first time, gives eligible Medicare Part D members access to Zepbound (KwikPen) for obesity at a flat $50 copay per 30-day supply. The program runs through December 31, 2027. You must be enrolled in a participating Part D plan, and you generally need a BMI of 35 or higher, or a BMI of 30 to 34.99 with a qualifying condition. Important: the $50 copay operates outside the normal Part D benefit, so it does not count toward your deductible or the $2,100 annual out-of-pocket cap, and single-dose vials are excluded.

sections.h2: Which GLP-1 drugs does Medicare cover in 2026?
sections.body: Medicare Part D can cover GLP-1 drugs when they are prescribed for an approved, non-weight-loss medical use and your plan lists them. Ozempic and Mounjaro may be covered for type 2 diabetes. Wegovy may be covered to reduce the risk of cardiovascular death, heart attack, or stroke in adults with established cardiovascular disease who are overweight or obese. Zepbound may be covered for obstructive sleep apnea. None of these are covered for weight loss alone outside the GLP-1 Bridge. Most plans also apply prior authorization, step therapy, or quantity limits, so two enrollees on the same drug can pay very different amounts.

sections.h2: What will I actually pay for covered drugs in 2026?
sections.body: In 2026, every Medicare Part D plan caps your annual out-of-pocket spending on covered drugs at $2,100. Once you hit that ceiling, covered prescriptions cost you nothing for the rest of the year. Insulin is capped at $35 (or less) per month with no deductible. Negotiated prices from the Medicare Drug Price Negotiation Program also took effect January 1, 2026; for example, Eliquis dropped to a $231 negotiated 30-day price, a 56% cut versus 2023. Your share still depends on your plan's tier, deductible, and pharmacy. A licensed specialist at 1-800-MEDIGAP can run your exact drug list against plans for free.

sections.h2: How do I find a plan that covers my medications?
sections.body: The single most important step is matching your specific prescriptions to a plan's formulary before you enroll. Two Part D or Medicare Advantage plans can treat the same drug very differently, one covering it on a low tier and another not at all. Bring your full medication list, including doses, and a licensed agent can compare formularies, prior-authorization rules, and total annual cost. This is exactly what 1-800-MEDIGAP does at no charge. One toll-free call, 1-800-633-4427, connects you with a specialist who can review GLP-1 coverage, the new Bridge program, insulin caps, and Medigap options side by side.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover Zepbound for weight loss?
faqs.a: Not for weight loss alone. Medicare Part D is barred by law from covering drugs used solely for weight loss. Zepbound may be covered when prescribed for an approved condition like obstructive sleep apnea, or accessed for obesity through the temporary GLP-1 Bridge program at a $50 copay starting July 1, 2026.

faqs.q: Who qualifies for the Medicare GLP-1 Bridge program?
faqs.a: You must be in a participating Part D plan and generally need a BMI of 35 or higher, or a BMI of 30 to 34.99 plus a qualifying condition such as diastolic heart failure or uncontrolled high blood pressure. The program runs July 1, 2026 through December 31, 2027 and charges a $50 copay per 30-day Zepbound KwikPen supply.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover Ozempic in 2026?
faqs.a: Medicare Part D may cover Ozempic when it is prescribed for type 2 diabetes and listed on your plan's formulary. It is not covered for weight loss. Your plan may also require prior authorization or step therapy, so confirm your specific coverage by calling 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: Does the GLP-1 Bridge copay count toward my Part D out-of-pocket cap?
faqs.a: No. The $50 Bridge copay operates outside the standard Part D benefit, so it does not count toward your annual deductible or the $2,100 out-of-pocket maximum. Drugs covered normally under Part D still count toward that $2,100 cap, after which covered prescriptions cost nothing.

faqs.q: How much is insulin under Medicare in 2026?
faqs.a: In 2026, a one-month supply of each covered insulin is capped at $35, or less, with no deductible applied to insulin. This applies in every coverage phase and to insulin used in pumps under Part B, where coinsurance also cannot exceed $35 per month.

faqs.q: What is the Medicare Part D out-of-pocket cap in 2026?
faqs.a: In 2026, Medicare Part D caps your out-of-pocket spending on covered drugs at $2,100 for the year. Once you reach that limit, you pay nothing for covered prescriptions for the rest of the calendar year. The cap applies to all Part D and Medicare Advantage drug plans.

faqs.q: Does Medicare Advantage cover GLP-1 weight-loss drugs?
faqs.a: Medicare Advantage plans follow the same federal rules as Part D, so they cannot cover GLP-1 drugs for weight loss alone. They may cover them for approved medical uses like diabetes or cardiovascular risk reduction, and participating plans can offer the GLP-1 Bridge. Coverage and costs vary by plan and formulary.

faqs.q: How can 1-800-MEDIGAP help with my drug coverage?
faqs.a: Licensed specialists at 1-800-MEDIGAP compare your exact medication list against Part D and Medicare Advantage formularies for free, checking GLP-1 rules, the Bridge program, insulin caps, and total annual cost. One call to 1-800-633-4427 helps you find a plan that covers your prescriptions.

sections.h2: When does Medicare cover Zepbound?
sections.body: Medicare Part D cannot pay for Zepbound (tirzepatide) when it is prescribed only for weight loss, because federal law excludes weight-loss-only drugs. However, a Part D plan that lists Zepbound on its formulary may cover it for an approved medical indication, such as moderate-to-severe obstructive sleep apnea in adults with obesity. Coverage is indication-specific, so the diagnosis on your prescription is decisive. Many plans also require prior authorization. To confirm whether your plan covers Zepbound for your situation, call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 for a free formulary check.

sections.h2: How does the GLP-1 Bridge change Zepbound access in 2026?
sections.body: Beginning July 1, 2026, CMS launched a temporary GLP-1 Bridge demonstration that, for the first time, lets eligible Medicare Part D members get Zepbound (KwikPen) for obesity at a flat $50 copay per 30-day supply, running through December 31, 2027. To qualify you generally need a BMI of 35 or higher, or a BMI of 30 to 34.99 with a condition like diastolic heart failure or uncontrolled high blood pressure. The $50 copay does not count toward your deductible or the $2,100 out-of-pocket cap, and single-dose vials are not included.

sections.h2: What will Zepbound cost me on Medicare?
sections.body: Your Zepbound cost depends on the path. Through the GLP-1 Bridge, eligible members pay a flat $50 per 30-day KwikPen supply. When Zepbound is covered under standard Part D for an approved medical condition, you pay your plan's tier cost-sharing, which counts toward the 2026 $2,100 annual out-of-pocket cap. Without coverage, the cash price runs many hundreds of dollars a month. Because plans differ widely, a free review at 1-800-MEDIGAP can show you the lowest-cost route for your specific diagnosis and plan.

faqs.q: Will Medicare cover Zepbound for sleep apnea?

sections.h2: When does Medicare cover Ozempic?
sections.body: Medicare Part D may cover Ozempic (semaglutide) when it is prescribed to treat type 2 diabetes and your plan lists it on its formulary. Because Ozempic is FDA-approved for diabetes, not weight loss, plans can pay for it under that diagnosis. Medicare cannot cover it when the sole purpose is weight loss. Many plans apply prior authorization, step therapy, or quantity limits, so the same drug can be easy to get on one plan and restricted on another. A free formulary check at 1-800-MEDIGAP confirms your specific coverage.

sections.h2: What does Ozempic cost on Medicare in 2026?

Once you hit $2,100 in covered drug costs, you pay nothing more for covered prescriptions that year. Exact monthly cost varies by plan tier, deductible, and pharmacy. Because two plans can price the same drug very differently, comparing formularies before you enroll is the best way to lower your cost. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a free side-by-side review.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover Ozempic for weight loss?
faqs.a: No. Medicare Part D is barred by law from covering drugs used only for weight loss, and Ozempic is FDA-approved for type 2 diabetes, not weight loss. It may be covered when prescribed for diabetes and listed on your plan's formulary.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover Ozempic for diabetes?
faqs.a: Yes, potentially. Medicare Part D plans may cover Ozempic when it is prescribed for type 2 diabetes and appears on the plan's formulary. Coverage often comes with prior authorization or step therapy requirements, which vary by plan.

faqs.q: Why does my plan require prior authorization for Ozempic?
faqs.a: Many Part D plans use prior authorization to confirm Ozempic is being prescribed for an approved use like type 2 diabetes before they pay. Your doctor submits documentation, and the plan reviews it. Rules vary, so call 1-800-MEDIGAP to understand your plan's requirements.

sections.h2: When does Medicare cover Wegovy?
sections.body: Medicare Part D may cover Wegovy (semaglutide) when it is prescribed to reduce the risk of cardiovascular death, heart attack, or stroke in adults who have established cardiovascular disease and are overweight or obese, provided your plan lists it on its formulary. This cardiovascular indication is the route to coverage, because Medicare cannot pay for Wegovy when the sole purpose is weight loss. Plans frequently require prior authorization to verify the diagnosis. To check whether your plan covers Wegovy for your condition, call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 for a free review.

sections.h2: What does Wegovy cost on Medicare in 2026?
sections.body: When Wegovy is covered for its cardiovascular indication, you pay your plan's formulary tier cost-sharing, which counts toward the 2026 Part D $2,100 annual out-of-pocket cap. After reaching that cap, covered prescriptions cost nothing for the rest of the year. If you do not meet the cardiovascular criteria, Wegovy is generally not covered, and cash prices run many hundreds of dollars monthly. Because plan formularies and prior-authorization rules differ, a free comparison at 1-800-MEDIGAP helps you find the lowest-cost covered option.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover Wegovy for weight loss?
faqs.a: No. Medicare Part D cannot cover Wegovy when the only purpose is weight loss. It may be covered when prescribed to reduce cardiovascular death, heart attack, or stroke risk in adults with established cardiovascular disease who are overweight or obese, if the plan lists it.

faqs.q: What is Wegovy's cardiovascular indication?
faqs.a: Wegovy is FDA-approved to reduce the risk of cardiovascular death, heart attack, and stroke in adults with established cardiovascular disease who are overweight or obese. This approved medical use is what allows Medicare Part D plans to cover it, typically with prior authorization.

faqs.q: Can I get Wegovy through the Medicare GLP-1 Bridge?
faqs.a: The 2026 GLP-1 Bridge program covers Zepbound (KwikPen) for obesity, not Wegovy. Wegovy coverage on Medicare comes through standard Part D for its cardiovascular indication. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to review which GLP-1 options fit your situation.

faqs.q: Does my plan require prior authorization for Wegovy?
faqs.a: Often yes. Many Part D plans require prior authorization to confirm Wegovy is prescribed for the approved cardiovascular indication before they cover it. Your doctor submits documentation for review. Rules vary by plan, so confirm yours with 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.a: A licensed specialist at 1-800-MEDIGAP can check your Part D plan's formulary and prior-authorization rules against your diagnosis for free. Call 1-800-633-4427 to compare plans and find coverage for Wegovy.

sections.h2: When does Medicare cover Mounjaro?
sections.body: Medicare Part D may cover Mounjaro (tirzepatide) when it is prescribed to treat type 2 diabetes and your plan lists it on its formulary. The large majority of commercial and Part D plans cover Mounjaro for diabetes. As with other GLP-1 drugs, Medicare cannot pay for it when the only purpose is weight loss; for obesity, the separate 2026 GLP-1 Bridge covers Zepbound, which shares the same active ingredient. Plans often apply prior authorization, step therapy, or quantity limits. A free formulary check at 1-800-MEDIGAP confirms your specific coverage.

sections.h2: What does Mounjaro cost on Medicare in 2026?
sections.body: When Mounjaro is covered for diabetes, you pay your plan's cost-sharing for its formulary tier, and that spending counts toward the 2026 Part D $2,100 annual out-of-pocket cap. Once you reach $2,100 in covered drug costs, covered prescriptions cost nothing for the rest of the year. Your exact monthly cost depends on plan tier, deductible, and pharmacy. Because plans price the same drug differently, comparing formularies before enrolling can meaningfully lower your cost. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a free side-by-side review.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover Mounjaro for weight loss?
faqs.a: No. Medicare Part D cannot cover Mounjaro when the sole purpose is weight loss. It may be covered when prescribed for type 2 diabetes and listed on your plan's formulary. For obesity, the 2026 GLP-1 Bridge covers Zepbound, which uses the same active ingredient.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover Mounjaro for diabetes?
faqs.a: Yes, often. Most Part D plans cover Mounjaro for people with type 2 diabetes when it appears on the plan's formulary. Coverage frequently comes with prior authorization, step therapy, or quantity limits that vary by plan.

faqs.q: Is Mounjaro the same as Zepbound?
faqs.a: Mounjaro and Zepbound both contain tirzepatide, but they are approved for different uses. Mounjaro is approved for type 2 diabetes; Zepbound is approved for weight management and sleep apnea. Medicare coverage follows each drug's approved indication, so the rules differ.

sections.h2: How does Medicare cover Eliquis?
sections.body: Medicare Part D plans generally cover Eliquis (apixaban), an anticoagulant used to prevent strokes and blood clots, typically on a preferred-brand tier. Unlike weight-loss drugs, Eliquis treats approved medical conditions, so coverage is standard, though each plan sets its own tier and cost-sharing. Eliquis was also among the first 10 drugs in the Medicare Drug Price Negotiation Program, and its lower negotiated price took effect January 1, 2026. To confirm your plan covers Eliquis and see your tier, call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 for a free check.

sections.h2: What does Eliquis cost on Medicare in 2026?
sections.body: Under the Medicare Drug Price Negotiation Program, Eliquis carries a $231 negotiated price for a 30-day supply in 2026, about 56% lower than its 2023 price. What you actually pay still depends on your plan's deductible and tier cost-sharing, but all covered spending counts toward the 2026 Part D $2,100 annual out-of-pocket cap. After you hit $2,100, covered prescriptions cost nothing for the rest of the year. Because plans differ, a free review at 1-800-MEDIGAP can identify the lowest-cost plan for your medication list.

faqs.q: Is Eliquis covered by Medicare Part D?
faqs.a: Yes. Medicare Part D plans generally cover Eliquis, typically on a preferred-brand tier, because it treats approved medical conditions like stroke and clot prevention. Your exact cost depends on your plan's deductible and tier cost-sharing.

faqs.q: What is the 2026 Medicare negotiated price for Eliquis?
faqs.a: Eliquis was one of the first 10 drugs negotiated under the Medicare Drug Price Negotiation Program. Its negotiated price is $231 for a 30-day supply effective January 1, 2026, roughly 56% lower than the 2023 price. Your plan cost-sharing applies on top of this.

faqs.q: Why did Eliquis get cheaper on Medicare in 2026?
faqs.a: The Inflation Reduction Act created the Medicare Drug Price Negotiation Program, and Eliquis was among the first 10 drugs negotiated. The negotiated price took effect January 1, 2026, lowering costs for the millions of Medicare enrollees who use it.

faqs.q: How much will I pay for Eliquis in 2026?
faqs.a: Your cost depends on your plan's deductible and tier, but the new $231 negotiated 30-day price lowers the baseline, and all covered spending counts toward the 2026 $2,100 Part D out-of-pocket cap. After reaching $2,100, covered prescriptions cost nothing.

sections.h2: Does Medicare cover weight-loss drugs in 2026?
sections.body: By law, Medicare Part D cannot cover any drug used only for weight loss, a rule that still stands in 2026. The major change is the CMS GLP-1 Bridge, a temporary demonstration effective July 1, 2026 that lets eligible Part D members access Zepbound (KwikPen) for obesity at a $50 copay per 30-day supply through December 31, 2027. Separately, GLP-1 drugs can be covered under standard Part D for approved medical uses, such as Ozempic and Mounjaro for diabetes or Wegovy for cardiovascular risk. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to see which path fits you.

sections.h2: Who qualifies for weight-loss drug coverage under the GLP-1 Bridge?
sections.body: To use the 2026 GLP-1 Bridge, you must be enrolled in a participating Medicare Part D plan and generally have a BMI of 35 or higher, or a BMI of 30 to 34.99 with a qualifying condition such as diastolic heart failure or uncontrolled high blood pressure. The program covers Zepbound KwikPen for a flat $50 copay per 30-day supply but excludes single-dose vials. Importantly, that copay does not count toward your deductible or the 2026 $2,100 out-of-pocket cap, because it runs outside the standard Part D benefit.

sections.h2: How do approved medical uses change coverage?
sections.body: Even outside the Bridge, GLP-1 drugs can be covered when prescribed for an approved condition Medicare already pays for. Ozempic and Mounjaro may be covered for type 2 diabetes; Wegovy may be covered to reduce cardiovascular death, heart attack, or stroke risk in qualifying adults; and Zepbound may be covered for obstructive sleep apnea. Coverage always depends on the diagnosis and your plan's formulary, and plans often require prior authorization. A free review at 1-800-MEDIGAP matches your prescriptions and diagnoses to plans that cover them.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover weight-loss drugs in 2026?
faqs.a: Not for weight loss alone under standard Part D. But the new CMS GLP-1 Bridge, effective July 1, 2026 through December 31, 2027, lets eligible enrollees get Zepbound (KwikPen) for obesity at a $50 copay per 30-day supply.

faqs.q: Who qualifies for the 2026 GLP-1 Bridge?
faqs.a: You must be in a participating Part D plan and generally need a BMI of 35 or higher, or 30 to 34.99 with a qualifying condition like diastolic heart failure or uncontrolled high blood pressure. The program charges a $50 copay per 30-day Zepbound KwikPen supply.

faqs.q: Which weight-loss drugs are covered under the Bridge?
faqs.a: The 2026 GLP-1 Bridge demonstration covers Zepbound (KwikPen) for obesity. It does not cover single-dose vials, and other GLP-1 drugs like Wegovy are not part of the Bridge, though they may be covered under standard Part D for approved medical uses.

faqs.q: Does the GLP-1 Bridge copay count toward my drug cap?
faqs.a: No. The $50 Bridge copay operates outside the standard Part D benefit, so it does not count toward your deductible or the 2026 $2,100 out-of-pocket maximum. Drugs covered normally under Part D do count toward that cap.

sections.h2: How does Medicare cover insulin in 2026?
sections.body: Medicare covers insulin through Part D for most enrollees and through Part B when insulin is used in an insulin pump. Under the Inflation Reduction Act, a one-month supply of each covered insulin product is capped at $35 or less in 2026, with no deductible applied to insulin. This $35 ceiling holds across every Part D coverage phase, so you pay no more than $35 even before meeting a deductible. The benefit applies to all Part D enrollees, including those with Extra Help, whose costs may be even lower. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to confirm your insulin is covered.

sections.h2: What about insulin used in a pump?
sections.body: Insulin used in an insulin pump is covered under Medicare Part B rather than Part D. Part B uses coinsurance instead of a flat copay, but in 2026 the coinsurance for insulin still cannot exceed $35 for a one-month supply. So whether your insulin runs through Part D or a Part B pump, your monthly cost for each covered insulin is held to $35 or less. If you are unsure which part of Medicare covers your insulin, a free review at 1-800-MEDIGAP can clarify your coverage and costs.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover insulin?
faqs.a: Yes. Medicare covers insulin through Part D and, for insulin used in pumps, through Part B. In 2026, a one-month supply of each covered insulin is capped at $35 or less with no deductible applied to insulin.

faqs.q: How much does insulin cost on Medicare in 2026?
faqs.a: A one-month supply of each covered insulin product is capped at $35 or less in 2026, with no deductible. The cap applies in every Part D coverage phase and to insulin used in pumps under Part B, where coinsurance also cannot exceed $35 per month.

faqs.q: Does the insulin deductible apply in 2026?
faqs.a: No. The 2026 $35 insulin cap applies with no deductible, meaning you pay no more than $35 for a one-month supply of each covered insulin even before meeting your Part D deductible, in every coverage phase.

faqs.q: Is insulin for pumps covered differently?
faqs.a: Yes. Insulin used in an insulin pump is covered under Part B, which uses coinsurance rather than a copay. In 2026, that coinsurance still cannot exceed $35 per month, so your monthly cost is capped just like Part D insulin.

faqs.q: How do I find a plan that covers my insulin?
faqs.a: A licensed specialist at 1-800-MEDIGAP can confirm your specific insulin is on a plan's formulary and apply the $35 cap to your costs, all for free. Call 1-800-633-4427 to compare your options.

page.metaDescription: The Medicare $35 insulin cap continues in 2026 with no deductible. See how it works and get free plan help at 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: The Medicare $35 insulin cap limits cost-sharing for a one-month supply of each covered insulin to $35 or less with no deductible. Created by the Inflation Reduction Act, it continues in 2026 and applies to all Part D enrollees and to Part B pump insulin.
page.intro: The $35 insulin cap is one of the most valuable Medicare changes for seniors with diabetes. Here is exactly how it works in 2026.
page.heroSubhead: Everything you need to know about the Medicare $35 insulin cap from licensed specialists at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: What is the Medicare $35 insulin cap?
sections.body: The $35 insulin cap, created by the Inflation Reduction Act, limits your cost-sharing for a one-month supply of each covered insulin to $35 or less, with no deductible applied to insulin. It continues in full force in 2026. For 2026, the cap is calculated as the lesser of $35, 25% of the maximum fair price for a negotiated product, or 25% of the negotiated price, so your cost could be even below $35. The cap applies to all Part D enrollees, including those with Extra Help. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to confirm it applies to your insulin.

sections.h2: Does the cap apply to insulin pumps and every coverage phase?
sections.body: Yes on both counts. The $35 cap holds in every Part D coverage phase, so you never pay more than $35 for a one-month supply of each covered insulin, even before meeting your deductible. It also extends to insulin used in insulin pumps, which is covered under Part B. Part B uses coinsurance instead of a copay, but in 2026 that coinsurance for insulin cannot exceed $35 per month. Whether your insulin is billed under Part D or Part B, your monthly cost is protected. A free check at 1-800-MEDIGAP confirms your coverage.

faqs.q: What is the Medicare $35 insulin cap?
faqs.a: It is a limit, created by the Inflation Reduction Act, that caps cost-sharing for a one-month supply of each covered insulin at $35 or less with no deductible. It continues in 2026 and applies to all Part D enrollees and to Part B pump insulin.

faqs.q: Does the $35 insulin cap continue in 2026?
faqs.a: Yes. The $35 insulin cap continues in 2026. For 2026 the amount is the lesser of $35, 25% of the maximum fair price for a negotiated insulin, or 25% of the negotiated price, so some enrollees pay even less than $35.

faqs.q: Does a deductible apply to the $35 insulin cap?
faqs.a: No. The $35 cap applies with no deductible. You pay no more than $35 for a one-month supply of each covered insulin in every Part D coverage phase, including before you meet your plan's deductible.

faqs.q: Does the $35 cap apply to insulin pumps?
faqs.a: Yes. Insulin used in an insulin pump is covered under Part B, which uses coinsurance. In 2026 that coinsurance for insulin cannot exceed $35 per month, so pump insulin is protected just like Part D insulin.

faqs.q: Who qualifies for the $35 insulin cap?
faqs.a: All Medicare Part D enrollees qualify, including those receiving Extra Help, who may pay even less. To confirm the cap applies to your specific insulin and plan, call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 for a free review.

children.keyword: medicare cover semaglutide for weight loss

page.metaTitle: Medicare Cover Semaglutide for Weight Loss? | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Does Medicare cover semaglutide for weight loss? Not alone, but there are covered paths in 2026. Free help at 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.h1: Medicare Cover Semaglutide for Weight Loss
page.quickAnswer: Medicare Part D cannot cover semaglutide (Ozempic, Wegovy, Rybelsus) for weight loss alone. It may be covered for approved uses: Ozempic for type 2 diabetes, or Wegovy to reduce cardiovascular death, heart attack, or stroke risk in qualifying adults, if your plan lists it.
page.intro: Semaglutide is the active ingredient in Ozempic and Wegovy, and Medicare's coverage depends entirely on the approved use, not weight loss. Here is the 2026 reality.
page.heroSubhead: Honest answers on Medicare and semaglutide for weight loss from licensed specialists at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: Does Medicare cover semaglutide for weight loss?
sections.body: No. Medicare Part D is prohibited by law from covering any drug used only for weight loss, and that includes semaglutide prescribed solely for that purpose. Semaglutide is sold as Ozempic and Rybelsus for type 2 diabetes and as Wegovy for weight management and cardiovascular risk. A Part D plan may cover Ozempic for diabetes, or Wegovy to reduce the risk of cardiovascular death, heart attack, or stroke in adults with established cardiovascular disease who are overweight or obese, if the plan lists it. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to see whether a covered path fits your diagnosis.

sections.h2: What are my covered options for semaglutide in 2026?
sections.body: Coverage hinges on the approved indication. If you have type 2 diabetes, a plan may cover Ozempic or Rybelsus, often with prior authorization. If you have established cardiovascular disease and are overweight or obese, a plan may cover Wegovy for cardiovascular risk reduction. For obesity itself, semaglutide is not part of the 2026 GLP-1 Bridge, which covers Zepbound (tirzepatide). Because coverage depends on diagnosis and formulary, a free review at 1-800-MEDIGAP can identify which semaglutide or alternative GLP-1 your plan will actually cover, and at what cost.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover semaglutide for weight loss?
faqs.a: No. Medicare Part D cannot cover semaglutide when the only purpose is weight loss. It may be covered as Ozempic or Rybelsus for type 2 diabetes, or as Wegovy to reduce cardiovascular risk in qualifying adults, if your plan lists it on its formulary.

faqs.q: Is Ozempic semaglutide covered by Medicare?
faqs.a: Ozempic is a brand of semaglutide. Medicare Part D may cover it when prescribed for type 2 diabetes and listed on your plan's formulary, often with prior authorization. It is not covered for weight loss alone.

faqs.q: Is Wegovy semaglutide covered by Medicare?
faqs.a: Wegovy is a brand of semaglutide. Medicare Part D may cover it to reduce the risk of cardiovascular death, heart attack, or stroke in adults with established cardiovascular disease who are overweight or obese, if your plan lists it. It is not covered for weight loss alone.

faqs.q: Can I get semaglutide through the 2026 GLP-1 Bridge?
faqs.a: No. The 2026 GLP-1 Bridge covers Zepbound (tirzepatide) for obesity, not semaglutide. Semaglutide coverage on Medicare comes through standard Part D for approved uses like diabetes or cardiovascular risk reduction.

faqs.q: How do I find a plan that covers semaglutide?
faqs.a: A licensed specialist at 1-800-MEDIGAP can match your diagnosis and prescription to plans that cover Ozempic, Wegovy, or Rybelsus for free, and explain prior-authorization rules. Call 1-800-633-4427 to compare your options.
